replies.content:
Loki's a tough one to know what to think/do about!

I have read some folks from whom I've gotten valuable stuff (like Galina Krasskova and Raven Kaldera) who talk about worshipping Loki and (some of) the other Jotnar. Their arguments tend to be something along the lines of "overly vilifying these embodiments of natural forces is likely a Christian lens looking back on what was originally a more nuanced relationship between entities of different tribes/provenances". With Loki specifically, he was (at least for a time) counted among the Aesir, Odin's blood-brother. In the mythology, he is definitely necessary for several key events (getting the wall around Asgard built - and getting out of paying for it! - getting the wondrous belongings of the Gods built, and so forth).

All that being said, I personally have held off of any direct veneration of Loki or the other giants, in no small part because of JMG's comments about experiences with Loki worshippers. It's a topic I need to do more meditation on, but one thing that has struck me is the similarity between Loki as depicted (and even his being Odin's blood brother) and certain expressions of the Luciferian archetype (especially Milton and Goethe). I'm influenced here by Jordan Peterson's distillation of mostly Jungian analysis, where he points to the Lucifer of Paradise Lost as a proto-totalitarian, someone so convinced of his own intellect and conclusions that he literally rebels against the foundation of all of reality. Similarly, I tend to see Loki as a figure who is overly fond of his own creations/efforts. He and Odin share charactersitics of cleverness and agency, but Odin is characterized by willing self-sacrifice and a concern for the greater good, whereas Loki tends to only give up what he is made to, and to choose what is best for number one, in the short term. Maybe a necessary force in the world, but not necessarily one to actively call into my life.

That being said, I try not to offer any disrespect, as that just seems like a bad idea.\

I hope this helps give you some perspective, and I look forward to seeing what others say.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Some potentially useful resources, if you'd like to learn more:

1) Someone up-thread mentioned "Norse Mythology for Smart People", which has a pretty complete discussion of most of the Gods and myths and does a good job of explaining how he has come to the conclusions he has where it's not straightforward. From some updates on his website, he's apparently mostly lost interest in things Norse and is now diving into Gnosticism. If you prefer book form, his The Viking Spirit and The Love of Destiny are pretty good.

2) Rudolf Simek's Dictionary of Northern Mythology is an excellent scholarly resource that is especially helpful for looking up characters, places, items, and so forth.

3) Claude Lecouteux's Encyclopedia of Germanic Folklore, Myth, and Magic fulfills a similar role, but trades a bit less depth in the core material (the Eddas, basically) for more breadth, especially with more recent folklore. If I could only have one of the Dictionary or the Encyclopedia, I'd likely go for the Dictionary, but they complement each other nicely.

4) Also mentioned upthread, but Jackson Crawford has some excellent translations (his goal was to make them suitable for a student of mythology, rather than for a poet or linguist), and he has lots of Youtube videos if you prefer that method of engaging with stuff. Speaking of Crawford's translations, I have found the Voluspa incredibly rich fodder for meditation, and it is the main source on Ragnarok (but less about Loki, specifically, than some other poems)

5) Another Youtuber, Rune Rasmussen posts to the "Nordic Animism" channel and he takes an interestingly different point of view (he has a PhD in Religious Studies focusing on Afro-Brazilian religions). He has a video on Loki here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wnHra8lJC5w and one about Ragnarok where he connects it with ecological thinking here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a_chnIBCiZM

6) If you're looking for a point of view from some folks that actually do worship Loki, Raven Kaldera maintains https://northernpaganism.org/ which includes a number of online "shrines" for different deities. There's one for Loki here: https://www.northernpaganism.org/shrines/loki/index.html

I've gathered that Kaldera may have some beliefs/practices that I wouldn't follow or necessarily recommend, but in his writings, at least, he's open-minded and reasonable about differences of belief/practice.

Hope some of these help!

In the latest episode of his podcast The Hermetic Hour, Poke Runyon stated that each person has three guardian angels: the angel of one's ascendant, the angel of the planet that rules the ascendant - which are both specific to the present lifetime - and the angel that accompanies one throughout all one's lives.

For example, in the case of Jupiter, would the angel be Tzadkiel, or would it be another in the same lineage but more personal to the individual? In that case, how would I find that out for myself?

For the angel that guides one through all lifetimes, is there a way to discover that spirit's nature or name, other than direct contact?

If you can recommend a good online source for this, I'd be glad to find it and study up. As you might guess, I'm giving serious consideration to something like the Abramelin operation and have obtained a book on that process designed for pagans.

For anyone who wishes to check out the podcast, it's at BlogTalkRadio[dot]com[slash]the-hermetic-hour. Poke is a lot of fund and well worth listening to.

replies.title: Re: Guardian Angels
replies.content:
There are tables in some of the old books of magic for calculating the name of the angel who rules your ascendant. I think Agrippa gives one in the third of his Three Books, and some of the other Renaissance magical literature does the same. My book The Art and Practice of Geomancy gives a geomantic method to do the same thing, using a method I found in one of John Heydon's books.

The angel of the planet is the standard planetary angel, so Sachiel for Jupiter and so on. (Again, Agrippa or any of the other classic texts likns these.) The angel that guides you through all your lives, as far as I know, you can only learn about by contacting. The angel of your ascendant and the angel of your ruling planet, on the other hand, you can know about in advance, and working with them can get you in contact with the third angel.

comments.title: The Creator God and the Ineffable One
comments.content:
I've been following the essays of John Gilbert that you've published thus far. They contain much food for thought. In particular, I am wondering what is the relationship, if any, between a creator god and the ineffable One? I can conceive of the gods of a polytheistic pantheon as emanating from the One, but am not sure where a creator god fits in here. Any thoughts?

If any particular author is especially apposite here - Porphyry, Iamblichus, Plotinus, Hermes Trismegistus, perhaps - it would be nice to know; and also in which works and (if possible) in which chapters or passages the theme is addressed, in the event you happen to recollect such details.

replies.title: Re: The Creator God and the Ineffable One
replies.content:
The creator god in polytheist faiths is simply one of the gods who has the job of making stuff: Ptah in Egyptian mythology, for example. He's not the Great Big God of monotheism, just the deity who happens to make worlds. In some traditions, for example the Norse one, the job's shared out among three gods, Odin, Vili, and Ve:

Then Bur's sons lifted | the level land,
Mithgarth the mighty | there they made;
The sun from the south | warmed the stones of earth,
And green was the ground | with growing leeks.

In the Neoplatonist vision, the gods are the first emanations from the One, the powers of the Noetic world, and each reflects the One in a distinct way -- so the love-goddess reflects the One as loving union, and so on. The creator god, the craftsman who makes the world, reflects the One as bringing things into being. There's something in Proclus about this, but I'm sorry to say I don't remember where.

replies.title: Re: The Creator God and the Ineffable One
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: The Creator God and the Ineffable One
replies.content: My take is that a lot of what's said about the One Big God of monotheism is what Whitehead called "metaphysical flattery" -- piling on the superlatives without reference to anything but the desire to praise one's god. Yes, the monotheist faiths generally insist that their god is also the transcendent ground of being, and then have to explain how the transcendent ground of being can be angry or kind, choose one little ethnic group as his Chosen People, or what have you. Polytheism, by contrast, doesn't have to insist that a principle is also a person!

replies.title: Re: The Creator God and the Ineffable One
replies.content:
One can observe this gloss occurring in Pseudo-Dionysius, who by most accounts is heavily influenced by Proclus. He yet manages, IMHO, to thread the needle between The One—the ineffable and transcendent—and the "persons" of the Christian trilogy, the personifications of what he deems the Super-Essential Godhead. Dionysius manages to pull off a fair balancing act, in my view, that seems to have gotten lost over time as Big Monotheism pushed everyone else offstage.

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.title: Re: The Creator God and the Ineffable One
replies.content: Interesting -- since I'm not a Christian, I haven't studied ps.-Dionysius, and wasn't aware of this. I could see a Neoplatonist analysis along those lines working quite well -- if the One is seen as a wholly ineffable Godhead that manifests itself through personifications, that would allow the necessary qualitative difference between henadic and noetic worlds.

replies.title: Re: The Creator God and the Ineffable One
replies.content: For its brevity, the Divine Names packs a surprisingly dense amount of Neoplatonic theory into its pages (some culled almost verbatim from Proclus, but as a shortcut beats reading the commentary on Timaeus or the Platonic theology). There are even some passages that almost seem to equate the "angelic intelligences" with the henads. As I understand it, his work (and later reintroductions of it, such as that by John Scotus Eriugena) are largely the reason that the shadows of Neoplatonic ideas persist along the edges of Christian theology.

I will try to outline this from the perspective of Sacramental Christian Churches. You may or may not accept the objective correctness of the argument, but if you are to pass moral judgements of the actors you must take their internal figurations and motivations.

1. Consecrating Russia and Ukraine should be understood as Consecrating Russian and Ukrainian Churches, individual Christians and that which is legitimally of them. Territory, society, etc, is only consecrated in as much as it is part of the Christian population living there. AFAIK, non Christian nationals will not be directly affected (though they will get to benefit to the degree that their Christian neighbors will be affected).

The Moscow and Kiev Patriarchates see themselves as struggling with a nosy elder sibling that kept them under their thumb as children and now cannot get around to accept they all live independent lives. If I may quote again from the aforementioned source, this time John Paul the 2nd's words: “Consecrating ourselves to Mary means accepting her help to offer ourselves and the whole of mankind to him who is holy, infinitely holy; it means accepting her help—by having recourse to her motherly heart, which beneath the cross was opened to love for every human being, for the whole world—in order to offer the world, the individual human being, mankind as a whole, and all the nations to him who is infinitely holy”.

Leaving the... spiritual flattery aside. The vicarious form, when coming from a superior, is a commitment to guide the subject of the ritual towards establishing such connection with St. Mary. But as an equal, fellow Christian, this is little but a prayer that She may contact the subject and offer her help.

replies.title: Re: Further reflection on the mechanics of consecration

That's an important and relevant question. Prayer as an attunement of the self with the divine, using whatever forms and ceremonies are relevant to one's own religious beliefs, is always appropriate, and if the Pope and Catholics generally want to get down on their knees and pray for divine intervention in this very messy situation, I'm entirely in favor of that; it's not necessarily an inefficient alternative -- it just depends on what the divine has in mind, which may not be what you, or I, or the Pope have in mind.

And that's just it. The vision of Fatima requested a particular ritual at a particular time, if I understand correctly, which would assist the divine energies (and human beings can always assist or hinder those energies to a limited extent) to make certain changes in the human world. That wasn't done. Now the Pope has up and decided to do it on his own initiative, for a different purpose -- as you say, functioning as an arm of the EU. I doubt the Virgin will be especially receptive to this attempt to yank her around.

To answer your specific question, yes, a case could be made that it's a function of superstition, but I'd say first that it's a wholly over-the-top display of spiritual pride, of a sort that's embarrassingly common in the modern Catholic hierarchy: the insistence, in effect, that the Pope can tell God what to do. I'm not a Catholic, of course, but it seems to me that from a Catholic standpoint, the first requirement of any attempt to intervene spiritually in this situation is repentance and confession of one's sins, since (again, given Catholic theology) an event like this does not happen except in response to human wickedness, and the wickedness here is far from one-sided...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Owning shares in a company is owning part of the company, so yes, the karma comes with it. It's as though you hired somebody, in an arrangement where you know you're not going to have any supervision over them; they'd get most of the karma but you'd get some. That doesn't mean that owning stocks is a bad idea -- it means that you need to do your ethical due diligence, and choose companies in businesses that make good ethical sense to you. replies.title: Re: Planetary Days
replies.content:
Not JMG but:

The days are ordered the way they are because it's what naturally follows from the planetary hours, which are ordered in reverse order from the distance as seen from Earth: Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Sun, Venus, Mercury, Moon, then start over. If you set sunrise (planetary hour 1) on a particular day as the Sun, then the 8th, 15th, and 22nd are also the Sun. Therefore the 23rd hour is Venus, the 24th is Mercury, and the 25th - i.e., the first hour of the new day - is the Moon.

Do this repeatedly until you get the first hour of the day belonging to the Sun again, and you have our familiar 7-day week in its conventional order.

This works mathematically because 24/7 is an irreducible fraction. It doesn't work with a 9-day week, though, because 24/9 reduces to 8/3. If you kept the day at 24 hours, you'd end up repeating the same sunrise hour on a 3-day cycle, not a 9-day one.

JMG has suggested in The Twilight of Pluto that one possible solution would simply be to let the planetary days be the planets, that is, remove the Sun and Moon from the week in place of Uranus and Neptune. The week would then run Neptune - Jupiter - Mercury - Saturn - Venus - Uranus - Mars. Another option would be to change the number of hours in a day - literally anything not divisible by 3 would work.

replies.title: Re: Planetary Days
replies.content: Brendhelm's explanation is quite correct. Since the full integration of Uranus and Neptune into the human psyche is thousands of years away, it's quite possible that by the time it happens, the day will no longer be divided into 24 hours, so the math will work out in a different way...

replies.content:
D'oh. I made things way more difficult for myself than they needed to be. (If there's a MM bingo card for querents, something to that effect should be on it.) Thank you both!


That narrows the possibilities down quite a bit. If the 9-day week follows the same general pattern, the days would have to come from taking every Nth planet from the list: Neptune, Uranus, Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Sun, Venus, Mercury, Moon, Neptune..., where N can be 1, 2, 4, 5, 7, or 8 (relative primes of 9). Between those 6 potential orderings, and 9 potential ways for the new calendar to line up with ours, that's only 54 combinations. It's not every day that I get to make predictions thousands of years into the future that are that precisely defined!

-- A.B.C.

Dear JMG,
would you say that the secret of the temple and the leylines are connected?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It depends very, very, very much on what you mean by that many-headed term "ley lines." There are at least three things covered by the phrase, and two of them are connected to the temple technology I discuss in my book The Secret of the Temple, but the third is the one most people think of, and it's not.

1) The leys, to give them their proper name, are a network of ancient tracks marked by megalithic ruins found in northwestern Europe and a few other parts of the world. Alfred Watkins' book The Old Straight Track is still the best introduction to them. They aren't natural -- they were built by megalithic cultures thousands of years ago -- and they seem to convey an energy or influence along straight lines across country, especially at certain times of year. My take is that they're very closely connected to the oldest forms of the temple tradition.

2) The dragon paths or lung mei, to borrow a phrase from Chinese feng-shui, are natural channels of subtle energy that flow through the landscape. They aren't straight lines -- they move sinuously, like a Chinese dragon in flight, following ridgelines and watercourses. Any decent book on traditional feng-shui will tell you about the lung mei. My take is that they are natural channels of the energy used by the temple technology.

3) These two things have become muddled together by people armed with too many dowsing pendulums and too little common sense, resulting in claims that ley lines crisscross the entire planet in straight lines and all we have to do is dowse frantically to find the thirty-seven of them that intersect at the corner mailbox. These have nothing to do with the temple tradition, or with anything else except some future second volume of that famous book Extraordinary Popular Delusions and the Madness of Crowds.

I know that's probably much more info than you were looking for, but it's a matter that needs more attention than it usually gets.

replies.title: Re: Buzzards
replies.content:
In the US, when carrion eating birds hang around someone's house it usually indicates there is a gas leak. That's because that US scents natural gas used in homes with a chemical similar to the smell of rotting meat to make it easy to notice. Carrion birds can smell faint concentrations of this from a ways away and will stick around, thinking there's some free food to be found.

If your locality also scents gas in this way, you might want to notify the utility so they can come check it out.

Much better to find a low level leak now than have your basement suddenly explode.

comments.title: Zen body-being
comments.content:
I read Zen Body-Being by Peter Ralston recently and was blown away by the Jungian level of "making the occult palatable to materialists" approach and the wisdom (and usefulness) of the principles Peter is expounding. Peter became the first non-asian world champion in a martial arts tournament. The emphasis is on being *effective* and finding an "effortless power".

There's a big emphasis on "being in your body", *feeling* your body and so on. But since it's coming from an Eastern lens, there's also this sort of "keep a blank slate in your mind" aspect. I'm sure it works for some folks, but since my other practices are Western, maintaining this no-thought mindstate is going to be incoherent, right?

So my friend suggested looking toward Physical Culture. I think of Physical Culture as emphasizing strength, but not necessarily the dynamics and effective, coordinated movement.
John (and commentariat welcome!), can you recommend any books along the lines of above?

Bobby

replies.title: Re: Zen body-being
replies.content:
1) I like Ralston, and his books are worth reading; but I would be careful mixing his stuff with anything else. He includes a great deal of energy work but only rarely calls it that. The empty mind isn't incompatible with western material (in fact it exists in the western tradition under the terms "silent prayer" and "contemplation"). The premise in an eastern context, is that if you are thinking you should be thinking; if you are doing something else, you should be doing something else. If I remember correctly, Ralston explicitly teaches meditation in the same style as JMG; but as one of several practices all included under the term "study".

2) Physical culture is something of an odd term. As a movement, it provided the foundation first for modern gymnastics and later for weightlifting, then as it was dying out it provided the basis of the fitness movement of the 80s. The term has been picked up again and used in two very different fashions, one of which is another way of talking about weightlifting. Properly, it should emphasize health (and there are MANY facets to that idea), with strength being a particular expression of it.

The single best book on the subject, especially if you favor calisthenics over weights, is probably "Convict Conditioning" by Paul Wade. The marketing and publisher around it are rather sleazy. But the text is worth reading, and I strongly support the basic premise that the most effective ways of exercising have been put by the wayside in order to help sell equipment and gym memberships. My main caution is that some of the most valuable remarks in the book are almost throw-away single sentence remarks. Other than that, there aren't really single books that I like, though there are plenty of books that have a handful of good ideas in them. This field seems to be in need of some serious research, study, and eventually renovation.

replies.title: Re: Zen body-being
replies.content:

The empty mind isn't incompatible with western material

And my mind had skipped the double negative. I think our gracious host had warned against "empty mind" practices if not balanced with "full" mind (i.e. discursive meditation). Toward the end of Zen Body-Being, it seemed to me Ralston advocated for keeping an empty mind when using the body which is quite often! (or just as a constant baseline state... I just gave the book to a friend, so can't quote it). The was my only "red-ish flag" as I found the rest of the book quite compelling. Even the "empty mind" practice was compelling :D You don't think this level of "empty mind" would be at odds with the western approach?

I'm not trying to "mix systems" but if I take ideas from this eastern book for my body(s) and then study western cabala and so on... that probably counts as mixing systems, eh?

JMG, is there a corresponding "compelling idea" in the western philosophy with regard to:

The premise in an eastern context, is that if you are thinking you should be thinking; if you are doing something else, you should be doing something else.

Or is "think when you're thinking; do when you're doing" not in contradiction with western ideas?

Where is the boundary to be found for "don't mix systems"?

For example, one high-level suggestion of Ralston's is to reject dogma and experience things for yourself instead. That doesn't seem problematic in a western context, does it?

On a much lower level, he advocates for holding the body in a fully relaxed state (the equivalent of "empty mind" I suppose) as it leads to optimal physical performance. But to achieve that during physically demanding times, you've got to train to simply be in that state always. Seems like a good idea (and give me good results on the snowboard). What would the western approach be? Only to enter that state during physically demanding times?

Sorry for the mess of questions :). Hopefully it's clear what I'm getting at.

replies.title: Re: Zen body-being
replies.content:
1) In the Western tradition it's much less simple. Obviously you don't want internal chatter to distract you while you're doing things, but the mind has many ways of thinking and understanding that don't reduce to mental chatter.

2) I tend to draw it fairly narrowly. Find a set of practices that works for you and stick to it for a year and a day before trying to drag in anything else.

3) The problem is that most people who think they're rejecting dogma are actually rejecting new ideas so they can follow their existing habits of thought, and most people who think they're experiencing things for themselves are just replaying their own mental habits. Trying something completely different and unfamiliar, exactly as taught, is a potent learning tool, and the fact that it feels awkward and wrong -- as of course it will at first -- is a measure of just how habit-ridden you are.

4) A fully relaxed state is limp. The Western idea is to use the muscles you need, and keep that others in that nice poised state halfway between limpness and rigidity.

replies.title: Re: Zen body-being
replies.content:

But maybe I'm reading it wrong. This book is not his magnum opus, but his 15-years-later 'for the laymen' book. That could be it too.

Inspired by Ralston's idea, I said, "relax" as too many muscles tensed up in my leg when I went snowboarding. The muscles I needed stayed engaged and the ones I didn't slowly released. replies.title: Re: Zen body-being
replies.content:
Sorry for the late response and hopefully you get this:

1) This is an excellent ternary. I know a lama who would approve.

2) I really enjoyed Genevieve Stebbins. Her work seems to have interacted really badly with my Dolmen Arch practice though so I had to shelve it.

3) He does make this explicit in one of his other books. You appear to have an excellent handle on this.

4) No. When he says relax, he means relax. The problem is that there is some subtle depth to the idea that goes far beyond what most people are familiar with.

A common way of teaching it in the west is that you have relaxation, and you have contraction. In between, you have a state called either grace, poise, or engagement depending on the writer. This will get you were you want to go if you practice it. It is a very solid and workable approach; as well as the one I use in teaching. But in my mind, it really doesn't make what is happening clear. You aren't contracting the muscles, you are actually doing something else.

The sliding filament theory of muscular action, or the idea that that human movement is the result of muscle fiber's sliding into themselves and shortening in order to create motion; is accepted as how most people move most of the time. There is also a small body of evidence that human's can also move in a different way(s?). At the bare minimum; panidculation has been shown to engage the afferent nervous system, and the strength of the motion doesn't correlate with normal peripheral nervous system engagement.

The eastern method is to teach the person to relax. First relax to become limp, then relax in order to move. Once some degree of facility is attained with this, it becomes possible to induce motion by trying to "let" or "release" the body to do what you want it to. This results in the same thing as the western state of poise. Done properly, it comes with the sensation that the antagonist muscle is lengthening in conjunction with the agonists contraction. With time and practice, it can start to feel like fluid is allowed to run through the limb and cause motion.

comments.title: Hypnosis
comments.content:
JMG,

I saw an amazing magic show a few years ago, involving hypnosis and suggestion (at least I think that's what it was) - making people forget what they'd just done a minute ago etc and I got to wondering - what's the connection between hypnosis and suggestion and magic? It seems to like they are forms of magic basically, which means true magical rituals should be able to achieve everything the hypnotist did on stage and much more?

replies.title: Re: Hypnosis
replies.content:
Not JMG, obviously; but I do practice magic and used to practice hypnosis.

The short answer is that Hypnosis, Suggestion, and Magic all involve inducing controlled changes in consciousness. You are correct that magic can do everything Hypnosis can, and more.

Suggestion usually refers to a specific technique, also called "affirmation". Sometimes it refers to a particular psychological theory that the subconscious mind can accidentally be influenced by commentary and declarations that it is exposed to in the environment. As a technique, the recitation of a verbal formula to have a particular effect can be found in both Hypnosis and Magic.

Hypnosis is a surprisingly slippery word. Originally, it referred to a particular body of practice that resulted from a physician named James Braid trying to secularize the then popular system of occultism called "Mesmerism". Since then, this body of practice has been renamed "Hypnotherapy" and that is the term used in clinical literature on the subject. In this context, the term Hypnosis is reserved for the particular process or technique used to work on a patient.

Magic comes in a number of different styles. Hermetic magic is known for playing badly with Hypnosis. Rune magic seems to be compatible with some of its methods but not with others. The workings contained in the Druid Magic Handbook appear to be more or less fully compatible; though I did find hypnotic methods to be an unnecessary supplement to the work contained there in. I believe one of the past grand arch-druids of the AODA was a professional hypnotherapist, though I leave that to JMG to confirm. Most of Hypnosis is connected to what is sometimes called "the lower astral" or portions of the mind dealing in concrete but not necessarily fully conscious forms. In this regard, it is basically cheap sorcery from a magical perspective. It doesn't have high aims, but it can be put to good use.

If you are interested in Magic, anything written by our Host would be an excellent start. Dion Fortune was both a psychologist and a magician, so her work would likely be of interest to you as well.

If you are interested in Hypnosis, Leslie Lecron's Self Hypnosis is the classical starting point. Autogenics is a nearly identical discipline and may also be of interest.

If you are interested in Suggestion as a technique (Affirmation); it is simple enough for an online tutorial, such as this: https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/64539.html

replies.title: Re: Hypnosis
replies.content: Hypnosis descends from magic by way of Franz Mesmer's "animal magnetism" -- which was much richer and more interesting than hypnosis, btw. Yes, the effects you see in hypnosis are among the things that mages use, but (1) most magic is subtler, and (2) there are forces mages use that hypnotists know nothing about.

comments.title: Place & Energy in America
comments.content:
Hello,

1. A comment -

There was a fascinating discussion about the energies of place in America recently. It seemed from the discussion that there are 2 things going on: 1. inhabited spaces have accumulated toxic energies from psychic malaise in postmodern society, even where they are otherwise good in natural terms (e.g. Ashland OR was discussed), and 2. the wilder the area is, the less likely it is to be corrupted and magic generally is easier practiced there (e.g. northern Michigan).

Just wanted to add my 2 cents that I have also spent a fair amount of time all over America, and most cities have largely the same uncongenial energetic signature, and also, while I have never been to Michigan, I have spent a fair amount of time in the "hemiboreal" woods of Minnesota and Wisconsin, the same ecoregion in other words, and I agree that it is a place of spiritual grandeur. In Europe, Finns inhabited a similar region and they were known to be accomplished in the esoteric arts until long after most Europeans had abandoned them.

There aren't too many places even in the 3.1 million square miles of the lower 48 that haven't been affected in some way. But it does seem that large expanses of wild nature can be congenial. Other places I came across that had a benefic energetic signature were the vast woodlands of the Ozarks and the blooming deserts of Arizona. But it would seem that in the settled spaces, there is not much variation from the pattern of malaise -- mental and spiritual monoculture is as much a thing as the physical version seen in corn and soy plantations across America.

I can see why "healing the land" was a theme of your Secret of the Temple book as it's badly needed just now.

2. I did a couple of divinations of "where to go in the next year" (since it's around the time of the GD/astrological new year), first in general direction, second with regard to career ... Anyway they seem a bit ambiguous.

First had the form (where i am now - where to go - the means): Death - Sun - V Pentacles

The first two cards aren't too hard to interpret it seems. But, having V Pentacles in the "means" position is a bit odd, given the temptation to associate it with penury. Older interpretations, however, like MacGregor-Mathers and Waite have this as a card of love in some readings.

Second had the form of (option 1 - option 2 - means of decision): Tower - High Priestess - IX Pentacles

The two options were to return to the corporate world or go into business for myself. But again the means of decision here is not as clear.

Any thoughts on these?

Thanks!

-Deneb

replies.title: Re: Place & Energy in America
replies.content:
1) Thanks for these data points. Urban areas do vary, in my experience -- there are cities that are mildly grubby, and cities that are over-your-head deep in steaming oobleck -- but there's always some gunk, of course.

2) The first one says to me very clearly, "go cheap." Relocate the low-budget way, spending as little as you can, at least at first. The second -- well, that first card tells you that the corporate world is about to crash, and the third tells you that you have what you need and should rely on that. I'd definitely say the second option.

I wonder if you can help me untangle some triangles (as they inhabit 2 and 3D). I've been meditating on this, but haven't found a thread to follow:

We've got an upright equilateral triangle (UET) representing Gwyar (transformation and change and associated with water and the color blue) - and a UET symbolizing the 3rd gate of the Sphere of Protection/fire/south/red. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Triangles are tricky things, symbolically speaking. They represent energy and they represent form -- the triangle, for example is the geometric symbol of Binah, the third sphere of the Tree of Life, which corresponds to Saturn and is the sphere of form. A paradox? Sure, but a meaningful one.

There's also something else going on. The three divisions of the Oracle are also three grades of initiation. These three correspond, in sacred geometry, to the √3, √2, and √5 (and φ, the Golden Proportion, which derives from √5); the equilateral triangle is the first linear figure generated from the √3 proportion, so it belongs in the first degree, while generation and transformation are among the symbols of the √2 function.

I'm sure none of that makes things any clearer, but as you proceed with the work it should start to make sense...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"I'm sure none of that makes things any clearer, but as you proceed with the work it should start to make sense..."

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Is there a reason why you chose the proportions in the Ellipse card without any obvious ratios of φ? IIRC the right triangle between focus, center, and minor axis endpoint right triangle was 3:4:5, and e.g. not a Kepler triangle.

Also it's really big on the card. After just reading the text (without loading images), that surprised me when I first saw it, because I thought the intended symbolism had more to do with the patterns of flow around it, like an airfoil.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Because φ doesn't come into the sequence of cards until much, much later. If you examine the exercise assigned to that card you'll find that it doesn't specify any proportions at all -- just the relationship between the two foci and the perimeter. It's thus an intermediate stage between the circle and the vesica piscis, where the first of the three basic root functions, √3, first appears. φ isn't part of the sequence until after the double square of card 23, which gives the third root function √5 and thus allows φ to be generated.

The area around the ellipse isn't relevant to the symbolism -- in these early cards, we're still working solely with very simple combinations of points, lines, and arcs. The flow is shown in the way the distances to the two foci change as you go around the perimeter.

make that distinction) in which one sees the Light of the Cross in the color of a Sephirah, vibrates the God-Name of that Sephirah at the heart, then finishes the ritual as usual (though pointing to the feet when saying "Malkuth").

In your opinion, does this seem like a safe or good idea? Why or why not?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It doesn't strike me as a good idea because it'll tend to unbalance you toward the sphere in question. The whole point of the Cabalistic Cross is that it places you in balance.

comments.title: Balance and (Lack of) Backlash
comments.content:
Mr. Greer --

This week has been very enjoyable, with starting on the first part of the SoP. One day, I did try making the sphere as large as I could...which was a bad idea, as you might imagine. I got it able to cover most of the municipal block I live on, but that wore me out so much that I have no intention of doing that again. Another response has been more subtle...

1) I have felt, at best, uncomfortable with working with goddesses for the better part of a decade, and so my practice has run to the lopsided. But since starting the initial SoP exercises, I've felt able to work with goddesses again. Is that sort of balance common after beginning the SoP?

2) I have not had any magical response of any kind to the COVID-19 vaccine, have you run across anyone else with that reaction? Maybe it was because the only reason I took it was to graduate college? I've got no idea.

replies.title: Re: Balance and (Lack of) Backlash
replies.content:
1) It seems to help some people that way.

2) A fair number of people report no problems with it -- though of course quite a few people report the opposite. I'm glad to hear you're in the former category.
